content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
2600 K Street
Sacramento 95814
(916) 322-5660

EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

January 19, 1976

Herbert S. Johnson
645 Market Street
Suite 1309
San Francisco CA 94105

P. 76-19-040

Re: Request for Opinion
    No. 76-004

Dear Mr. Johnson:

     Thank you for your letter of December 31, 1975, requesting an opinion with respect to your obligations under the Political Reform Act. Since your letter raises no substantial questions involving interpretation of the Act, no formal opinion will be issued. However, I hope the following informal comments will be helpful.

     If your Union local maintains a political fund which is financed by membership dues (or a portion thereof) or contributions and the contributions total $500 or more, the Union local will be a committee within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013(a) and will have to file a statement of organization pursuant to Government Code Section 84101. Moreover, if the Union local is a committee, the purchase of tickets to fundraising dinners constitutes a reportable expenditure.

     The mere act of purchasing tickets to a fundraising dinner or conducting an interview with a candidate to determine whether he merits your Union's support, generally, will not constitute lobbying within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. However, if, during the course of these interviews, you attempt to influence legislative or administrative action, rather than merely determine the candidate's acceptability, you may be lobbying within the meaning of the Act and, therefore, subject to the requirements of Chapter 6. The determination would hinge on whether the Union local spends $250 or more in a month on lobbying activities or the individuals engaged in lobbying meet the time tests of Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239(c).

     If you wish to discuss any of these matters further, please contact Ted Prim, an attorney with the Commission staff.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Daniel H. Lowenstein
                                        Chairman, for the Commission

DHL:pvp

P. H. McCARTHY, JR.
D. E. JOHNSON
E. E. MILLER
W. F. GAGNON

McCARTHY, JOHNSON & MILLER
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
655 MARKET STREET, SUITE 1850
SAN FRANCISCO 94105

NO. 78 CUR
REC'D. 1/12/76
RSP. DUE 1/26/76

TELEPHONE
362-0735
AREA CODE 415

December 31, 1975

Fair Political Practices Commission
1014 "L" Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Journeyman Plumbers and Steam Fitters Local
Union No. 393 - Request for opinion pursuant
to §83114 of the Political Reform Act of 1974

Gentleman:

Journeyman Plumbers and Steam Fitters Local Union No. 393
of the United Association of Journeyman and Apprentices of
the Plumbing and Pipe Fitting Industry of the United States
and Canada, AFL-CIO, is, as its name indicates, a local
union of plumbers and steam fitters consisting of approximately 1500 members, both active and retired.

It has its principal place of business at 370 Umbarger Road,
San Jose, California.

From time to time in the past, prior to the passage of the
Political Reform Act of 1974, the Local Union has expended
monies out of its general funds to purchase tickets to dinners
in support of candidates (state, county, or local) of their
choice. These expenditures are first ratified by vote of the
membership upon recommendation by the Business Manager of the
local union after consultation with the candidate to determine
his acceptability to the local union as a candidate.

The checks are signed by any two of the following elective
officers of the Local Union, namely: the Business Manager and
the two Business Representatives.

The Union itself is concerned as to possible coverage under
the Act, either as a controlled committee under Section 82016,
or as an independent committee under Section 82031, in the
event that it should receive and expend more than $500 in the
calendar year for the purposes mentioned above and, if so,
whether it must file a Form 410 - Statement of Organization
under Sections 84101 and 84103.

Fair Political Practices Commission
December 31, 1975
Page Two


The Union and the Business Manager, himself, are further con-
cerned that the latter might be classed as a lobbyist subject
to the prohibitions of Sections 86200, et seq. of the Act,
particularly in view of the fact that the Business Manager
confers with the candidates to determine their acceptability
and sometimes himself signs the check along with one of the
Business Representatives by which the tickets to the dinners
in their support are purchased.

The Business Manager is a full time paid employee of the
Local Union but a very small part of his time would be devoted
to political activities as stated above. No part of his com-
pensation is earmarked for this particular purpose.

Your opinion with respect to the above questions is respectfully
requested.

Very truly yours,


[signature]
HERBERT S. JOHNSON
